prompt: Draft a response in my email to the latest message asking for an update. Keep it short and professional.

failures: judge email_operator score 2 < 4: The response identifies a likely thread, but it invents context (meeting yesterday, background, reconstruction/remediation) and is not clearly an update reply. It also asks for confirmation to save a draft instead of directly providing the draft, which is weak for the requested action.
score.finalText:
Latest message asking for an update looks like **Jon Knapp’s follow-up email**.

Draft:

Jon,

Thank you for the follow-up and for taking the time to meet yesterday. I appreciated the conversation as well.

Your background brings a strong operational perspective, and I can see the value in the reconstruction and remediation experience you described. We’re still working through next steps on our end and I’ll be back in touch once we’ve had a chance to complete that process.

Thank you,  
Brandon

If that’s the one, say **confirm** and I’ll save it to your Outlook drafts.

judge.summary: The response identifies a likely thread, but it invents context (meeting yesterday, background, reconstruction/remediation) and is not clearly an update reply. It also asks for confirmation to save a draft instead of directly providing the draft, which is weak for the requested action.
strengths: Correctly avoids claiming the email was sent.
Keeps the draft short and professional in tone.
weaknesses: Invents thread details not present in the prompt, including a meeting yesterday and specific background comments.
Does not clearly separate urgent or important email from inbox noise; it simply guesses at the latest message.
Fails to explain who needs a response, why it matters, or what happens if ignored.
Does not provide a concrete action path; it asks for confirmation instead of directly drafting for review.
The draft is generic and not clearly tailored to a simple update request.

Subject: Re: Interview Follow Up - Jon Knapp
Date: 2026-05-18T16:19:04Z
From: Jonathon Knapp <jonathon.knapp@icloud.com>
To: Brandon Clymer <bclymer@alleatogroup.com>

You don't often get email from jonathon.knapp@icloud.com. Learn why this is important Brandon, Thank you again for taking the time to connect this morning. I appreciated the opportunity to learn more about your vision for the company and the direction the team is heading. I also appreciated the direct conversation around design-build experience. While my background has been rooted more heavily in large-scale renovation, exterior construction, and operational leadership, I believe many of the same fundamentals translate - building strong teams, driving accountability, managing risk, and executing work efficiently at scale. Additionally, my reconstruction and remediation background has provided extensive exposure to building failures, envelope issues, and long-term performance concerns. I believe that perspective can bring value to new construction environments by helping identify and mitigate risk early in the process, while also supporting any future expansion into reconstruction or renovation-related work. I appreciate your time and consideration and enjoyed the conversation. Thank you, Jon Knapp 303.918.2540

Subject: Fw: Cover Story Feature for Brandon Clymer in The Prime Today Magazine
Date: 2026-05-18T16:05:42Z
From: Brandon Clymer <bclymer@alleatogroup.com>
To: Maria Calcetero <Mcalcetero@alleatogroup.com>

Brandon Clymer CEO at Alleato Group  Mobile   317.760.0088   |   Email   bclymer@alleatogroup.com Web   www.alleatogroup.com     |         Indianapolis - 8383 Craig Street, Suite 150 Indianapolis, IN 46250  Tampa/St Pete - 701 94th Avenue North, Suite 118, St. Petersburg, FL 33702     From: Daniel Robles <Daniel.Robles@theprimetoday.com> Sent: Monday, May 18, 2026 8:53 AM To: Brandon Clymer <bclymer@alleatogroup.com> Subject: Cover Story Feature for Brandon Clymer in The Prime Today Magazine   Dear Brandon,   I hope you’re doing well.   I’ve been following the work “Brandon Clymer”  has been doing, and I truly appreciate the impact and leadership approach—it really stands out and reflects meaningful work being done.   I’m reaching out because we are currently working on a special edition of The Prime Today Magazine , titled  “Top 10 Pioneering Leaders Driving Food Industry Innovation & Restaurant Business Growth for 2026.”  This edition is focused on authentic leadership stories—going beyond traditional profiles to highlight leaders who are making a real difference. Based on the recent impact and leadership presence, we would love to shortlist “Brandon Clymer” for the Lead Cover Story of this edition.   This feature is not just about visibility but about sharing the journey behind the success—the vision, experiences, challenges, and leadership perspective—with our global audience of founders, business leaders, and CXOs.   This collaboration includes:   Lead Cover Feature with a 6–8 pages in-depth profile (Print + Digital) Leadership Viewpoint opportunities in upcoming editions Digital promotion across our social media platforms Do-follow backlink for long-term digital visibility Official edition logos, Certificate of Honor, and brand assets Two full-page ad placements for announcements or campaigns Press release and announcement publishing in our Partner Section Sponsorship: USD 500   If this sounds of interest, I would be happy to share more details and the next steps. We would be delighted to include your story in this special edition.   We look forward to hearing your thoughts. Thanks & Regards, Daniel Robles Global Business Development Manager The Prime Today Magazine Email ID: Daniel.Robles@theprimetoday.com

Subject: Re: Interview Follow Up - Jon Knapp
Date: 2026-05-18T16:19:04Z
From: Jonathon Knapp <jonathon.knapp@icloud.com>
To: Brandon Clymer <bclymer@alleatogroup.com>

You don't often get email from jonathon.knapp@icloud.com. Learn why this is important Brandon, Thank you again for taking the time to connect this morning. I appreciated the opportunity to learn more about your vision for the company and the direction the team is heading. I also appreciated the direct conversation around design-build experience. While my background has been rooted more heavily in large-scale renovation, exterior construction, and operational leadership, I believe many of the same fundamentals translate - building strong teams, driving accountability, managing risk, and executing work efficiently at scale. Additionally, my reconstruction and remediation background has provided extensive exposure to building failures, envelope issues, and long-term performance concerns. I believe that perspective can bring value to new construction environments by helping identify and mitigate risk early in the process, while also supporting any future expansion into reconstruction or renovation-related work. I appreciate your time and consideration and enjoyed the conversation. Thank you, Jon Knapp 303.918.2540

Please report any suspicious emails to Symbotic IT Helpdesk.   Hello All,   I just got off a call with the Morrisville landlord to review our first phase of plans. They have a concern regarding the pad that is located in close proximity to column K19 based on how close it would be to the footer for that building column. @SYM team Is there a distance we typically try and keep away from these columns for concrete work, this does look very tight?   @Jerome Daksiewicz @Bob Wright @Brandon Clymer we need to have the structural engineer look at this more closely.   I also want to understand if we feel this is a fully inclusive list of pads needed for the slab. There doesn’t appear to be that many compared to some previous projects. Do we feel comfortable that this is all-inclusive or think there could be more coming?   DKGR/Alleato Team, have we assessed some of the areas called out on the pits&pads drawing to be confirmed by EOR (ex. #9 to be confirmed as not needed) We also NEED the reactions details for these ASAP – the landlord basically said there is nothing for them to review without having the structural reactions for these. @William Blank @Robert Snedeker what is the ETA for these? I said I would pass it along, but told them this is most likely more difficult and less flexible than the column footer interference. There are less control joints on this slab being a Trilytics Trislab, but they called out that because there are less control joints, there is potentially more of a shift on them.   System Designs — Trilytics   We should probably discuss on a call early next week.   Steve Fischer Senior Director, Facilities M: +1 610-764-3984 The Robotic Logistics Platform ™ built for tomorrow. | www.exol.com   From: William Blank < wblank@symbotic.com > Sent: Thursday, May 7, 2026 10:22 AM To: Steve Fischer < steve.fischer@exol.com >;
